title: Higgs Boson: How Scientists Proved That Particles Get Their Mass
description: How did physicists at CERN prove that an invisible field gives particles their mass? Read the story of the 2012 Higgs boson discovery and the mechanism behind all particle mass. Practice English vocabulary with instant word translations.
text:
The 2012 discovery of the Higgs boson at CERN matters because it confirmed a specific mechanism in the Standard Model of particle physics: particles get mass through their interaction with a field that fills all space. On 4 July 2012, the ATLAS and CMS experiments at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) announced a new particle with a mass near 125 giga–electron volts (GeV). Later measurements showed its properties match the long-predicted Higgs boson, the key sign of the Higgs field.

The idea behind the Higgs mechanism was developed in 1964 by several physicists, including Peter Higgs, François Englert, and Robert Brout. The problem was technical but fundamental: the theory that describes the weak nuclear force requires force-carrying particles, the W and Z bosons, to be massive, yet a basic version of the theory only works cleanly if those particles are massless. The Higgs mechanism solves this by using “spontaneous symmetry breaking,” where the laws keep a symmetry but the lowest-energy state of the universe does not. This allows the W and Z bosons to acquire mass while keeping the theory mathematically consistent.

In the Standard Model, the Higgs field is a quantum field that has a non-zero value even in empty space. Particles that couple strongly to this field behave as if they have more inertia, which we measure as mass. This does not mean the Higgs field “creates” matter; it changes how particles move and how they respond to forces. The W boson mass is about 80 GeV and the Z boson mass is about 91 GeV, while the photon has zero mass. The difference comes from how these particles interact with the Higgs field: W and Z couple to it, but the photon does not.

A quantum field can also have particle-like excitations. The Higgs boson is the detectable ripple of the Higgs field. Finding the boson was important because it is hard to measure the field directly; instead, physicists looked for the boson’s creation and decay in high-energy collisions. Without a Higgs-like particle, the Standard Model would fail to explain why the W and Z bosons are massive without breaking key principles such as gauge symmetry and unitarity, which protect the theory from giving impossible probabilities at high energies.

CERN’s LHC provided the needed energy by colliding protons at multi-tera–electron volt (TeV) energies. In 2012 it operated mainly at 7 and 8 TeV center-of-mass energy. ATLAS and CMS are independent, general-purpose detectors designed to reconstruct the spray of particles produced in each collision. Because a Higgs boson is extremely short-lived, the experiments searched for specific decay products. Two of the cleanest channels were Higgs decays to two photons (H → γγ) and to two Z bosons with one Z possibly “off-shell,” leading to four charged leptons (H → ZZ* → 4ℓ, where ℓ can be an electron or muon). Both experiments observed an excess of events around 125 GeV with a statistical significance near the 5-sigma level, the common threshold used in particle physics to claim a discovery.

Confirming the Higgs mechanism required more than seeing a bump in a mass plot. After 2012, the key tests were whether the new particle had the right quantum numbers and whether it coupled to other particles in the way the Standard Model predicts. Measurements showed it has spin 0, consistent with a scalar boson, and that it decays into combinations such as ZZ, WW, γγ, and fermion pairs in patterns consistent with Higgs couplings. A central prediction is that the Higgs couples more strongly to heavier particles. Experiments later observed Higgs decays to tau leptons (H → ττ) and to bottom quarks (H → bb), and they measured production modes such as vector boson fusion and associated production with a W or Z boson. These results supported the mass-coupling relationship that is built into the mechanism.

This discovery did not explain all mass in nature. Most of the mass of protons and neutrons comes from quantum chromodynamics: the energy of quarks and gluons confined inside hadrons, not directly from the Higgs field. However, the Higgs mechanism is still essential because it gives mass to the fundamental quarks and leptons and to the W and Z bosons, shaping nuclear reactions and the behavior of atoms. Its confirmation turned a key part of the Standard Model from an elegant idea into an experimentally verified description of how electroweak physics works.

Today, Higgs studies remain a main goal of the LHC because small deviations from Standard Model predictions could reveal new physics, such as additional Higgs bosons or links to dark matter. The 2012 result closed a major gap in a theory built through the 20th century, and it set a precise target for testing whether the Standard Model is complete or only an accurate approximation. That matters because understanding the origin of particle masses is not a detail: it determines which particles can exist, how forces act over distance, and why the universe has stable matter at all.

cardDescription: How the 2012 discovery of the Higgs boson at CERN confirmed that particles get their mass from an invisible field filling all of space.
